42MP/CÁC DẠNG CÂU HỎI TRONG ĐỀ THI/ĐỌC HIỂU/Thi Online Cách làm câu hỏi TRUENOT TRUENOT MENTIONED EXCEPT trong bài đọc ( Đề số 01).pdfok115 / 0 / 1515 / 15thpt_reading_passage:15missing:15
Passage passage-e327bd654f-01-33e04bb392 (15 questions)
Read the following passages and mark the letter A, B, C, or D on your answer sheet to indicate the best answer to each of the following questions. No animal species can survive indefinitely on the Earth. Centuries ago, species went extinct from natural causes, for example, they were unable to adapt to bad weather and other difficult conditions. However, animals are now dying out faster than ever because of human activity. It is estimated that, until the 18th century, one species disappeared from the Earth every four years. By the 19th century, this had increased to one species per year. By 1975, it was 1,000 species per year, and today, animals are disappearing at the alarming rate of more than 40,000 species per year. Many factors can cause an animal or plant species to become endangered, and one big one is the destruction of their habitats. Deforestation and soil, air, and water pollution are usually the main culprits. For example, the population of critically Sumatran orangutans is now less than 10,000 on their home island of Sumatra, Indonesia, due to deforestation and farming. Another cause of endangerment is humans exploiting wild animals. Uncontrolled hunting of whales in the last century, for example, resulted in many whale species becoming critically endangered. The high demands for animal parts stem from their use in foods or medicines or their value as decorative objects. For example, the ivory tusks of elephants are used to make jewellery, and the price is high enough that people risk being arrested and jailed to go after these animals. (Adapted from English Workbook 12)
Q1 Which of the following is NOT mentioned in paragraph 1 as one of the causes of animal extinction?
A. natural disasters | B. human activities | C. bad weather | D. difficult conditions
Ans: None (missing), ready=True, E2R.02, thpt_reading_passage
Q2 Which of the following is TRUE according to the passage?
A. The number of animal species going extinct per year has increased significantly over time. | B. The demand for ivory has decreased because it is illegal to hunt elephants. | C. All endangered species become extinct due to human activity. | D. Whales are no longer hunted for commercial purposes.
Ans: None (missing), ready=True, E2R.02, thpt_reading_passage
Q3 Which of the following is NOT TRUE according to the passage?
A. Some species have become endangered because humans hunt them for decorative items. | B. Sumatran orangutans are critically endangered due to deforestation and farming. | C. Whales are being endangered due to polluted oceans. | D. Pollution is one of the causes of habitat destruction.
Ans: None (missing), ready=True, E2R.02, thpt_reading_passage

A. English | B. Spanish | C. Assiniboine | D. Mandarin
Ans: None (missing), ready=True, E2R.02, thpt_reading_passage

Ans: None (missing), ready=True, E2R.02, thpt_reading_passage

A language holds cultural values and helps explain the world. | D. The last two speakers of Ayapaneco worked together to preserve the language.
Ans: None (missing), ready=True, E2R.02, thpt_reading_passage

43MP/CÁC DẠNG CÂU HỎI TRONG ĐỀ THI/ĐỌC HIỂU/Thi online Cách làm câu hỏi tóm tắt nội dung đoạn trong bài đọc hiểu (Đề số 01).pdfok420 / 0 / 2020 / 20thpt_reading_passage:20missing:20
Passage passage-b144c76dc3-01-fa354bca39 (5 questions)
1. For many years, people have believed that going to university is the best way to have a successful future. It offers students the chance to gain knowledge, build a career, and earn a good salary. However, not everyone chooses this path. In fact, more people today are starting to question whether university is truly necessary for success. Some individuals prefer to take a different route. Instead of spending several years in academic study, they learn skills through vocational training, online courses, or apprenticeships. Jobs in areas like technology, design, hospitality, or construction often do not require a university degree. These people focus on gaining practical experience, which is also highly valued in the job market. Another reason some people avoid university is the high cost. In many countries, tuition fees and living expenses are very expensive. Not attending university means avoiding student loans and starting to earn money earlier. For some, working while learning on the job is a more sensible and affordable option. In the end, success does not come from one single path. While university works well for many, it is not the only way to achieve goals. What matters most is understanding your strengths, interests, and what kind of life you want. Whether through university or other paths, people can build a meaningful and rewarding future by choosing what suits them best.
Q1 Which of the following best summarises paragraph 1?
A. University helps students earn more money, so most people still believe it is the only way. | B. Many still believe in university, but more people now question if it’s truly necessary. | C. Going to university is important for getting a good salary and becoming more knowledgeable. | D. People can build a career if they go to university and work hard to succeed.
Ans: None (missing), ready=True, E2R.08, thpt_reading_passage
Q2 Which of the following best summarises paragraph 2?
A. Some people learn practical skills and work in fields that don’t require a university degree. | B. Many students choose to study technology and design at university for a better future. | C. People can get jobs in hospitality or construction if they go to college or university. | D. Vocational training is easier than university, so more people now prefer it over academic study.
Ans: None (missing), ready=True, E2R.08, thpt_reading_passage
Q3 Which of the following best summarises paragraph 3?
A. Some people avoid university because it is costly and prefer earning money through work experience. | B. University students often pay high fees and take out loans to cover their living expenses. | C. Many people believe working while studying is more useful than going to university full time. | D. Not going to university helps students choose better jobs and avoid studying for too long.
Ans: None (missing), ready=True, E2R.08, thpt_reading_passage
Q4 Which of the following best summarises paragraph 4?
A. Many people believe that university is still the best way to have a successful future. | B. People must decide early if they want a university degree or a different career. | C. Knowing your goals helps you decide whether to study more or begin working right away. | D. Success depends on choosing a path that fits your strengths, goals, and personal interests.
Ans: None (missing), ready=True, E2R.08, thpt_reading_passage
Q5 Which of the following best summarises the passage?
A. Many people believe university guarantees success, but practical skills, job experience, and avoiding | B. Going to university is expensive, so people are choosing jobs in fields like design and technology | C. Although university offers knowledge and career opportunities, some people prefer to work in | D. Success today depends on choosing a path that suits your goals, but for most people, university remains
Ans: None (missing), ready=True, E2R.08, thpt_reading_passage
Passage passage-b144c76dc3-02-7c87af7581 (5 questions)
2. At Oakwood Elementary School, there is a special teaching assistant named Robo-Teach. This small, blue-and-white robot helps students with their lessons and makes learning fun. Robo-Teach can move around the classroom, answer questions, and even tell jokes to keep kids smiling. The students love how patient and kind the robot is, especially when they need extra help with maths or reading. Robo-Teach works with the teacher to make lessons more interesting. It can show videos on its small screen, quiz students with fun games, and even read stories aloud. The robot remembers each student’s name and knows which subjects they find tricky. This way, it can give them the right help when they need it. The teacher says Robo-Teach makes the classroom a happier and more organised place. The best part is that Robo-Teach never gets tired or frustrated. If a student doesn’t understand something, the robot will explain it again in a different way. It also cheers students on when they get answers right, saying things like, “Great job!” or “You’re getting better every day!” This makes the kids feel proud and excited to learn. Robo-Teach has become a favourite at Oakwood Elementary. Students look forward to seeing it every day, and even shy kids feel comfortable asking it questions. The robot may not replace human teachers, but it sure makes school a lot more fun and helps everyone learn better. Who wouldn’t want a robot friend like Robo-Teach?
Q6 Which of the following best summarises paragraph 1?
A. Robo-Teach is a friendly, mobile robot at Oakwood Elementary that helps students learn and brings | B. Robo-Teach assists students at Oakwood Elementary but mostly tells jokes and entertains kids when | C. Students at Oakwood Elementary use Robo-Teach for maths, though the robot cannot answer questions | D. Oakwood Elementary students enjoy learning with Robo-Teach, a blue robot who helps with reading
Ans: None (missing), ready=True, E2R.08, thpt_reading_passage
Q7 Which of the following best summarises paragraph 2?
A. Robo-Teach entertains students with games, videos, and stories to make school more fun and exciting. | B. Robo-Teach assists teachers by making lessons engaging and helping students learn based on | C. Robo-Teach plays games, reads stories, and remembers student names to improve classroom | D. Robo-Teach is a helpful school robot that reads aloud, plays games, and shows short videos.
Ans: None (missing), ready=True, E2R.08, thpt_reading_passage

Ans: None (missing), ready=True, E2R.08, thpt_reading_passage

Ans: None (missing), ready=True, E2R.08, thpt_reading_passage

Passage passage-b144c76dc3-03-04272a6b8d (5 questions)
3. Quan họ is a beautiful form of traditional folk singing from northern Vietnam, known for its gentle melodies and poetic lyrics. For many years, this art form was fading as younger generations became less interested. To keep quan họ alive, a special programme was created to teach and promote it. The goal is to make sure this important part of Vietnam’s culture does not disappear. The programme includes workshops where master singers teach young people the songs and techniques of quan họ. Schools and community centres also hold performances so everyone can enjoy this music. By making learning fun and interactive, the programme encourages children and teenagers to take part. Many young singers now proudly perform quan họ at festivals and competitions. Local leaders and artists are working together to support this revival. They organise events where quan họ singers perform in villages, reminding people of its beauty. Some groups even mix quan họ with modern music to attract younger audiences. Thanks to these efforts, more people are starting to appreciate this traditional art form again. The programme has brought new energy to quan họ, helping it stay relevant in today’s world. Families now sing these old songs together, and villages celebrate their cultural heritage with pride. By passing quan họ to the next generation, Vietnam ensures that this unique music will continue to be loved for years to come.
Q11 Which of the following best summarises paragraph 1?
A. A programme was launched to teach Vietnam’s traditional music, including melodies and songs like | B. Quan họ has poetic lyrics and melodies but isn’t popular with young people in Vietnam anymore. | C. Quan họ, a fading Vietnamese folk art, is being revived through a special teaching programme to | D. Quan họ is a traditional Vietnamese folk song once extinct, now taught through a cultural programme.
Ans: None (missing), ready=True, E2R.08, thpt_reading_passage

Ans: None (missing), ready=True, E2R.08, thpt_reading_passage

Ans: None (missing), ready=True, E2R.08, thpt_reading_passage

Ans: None (missing), ready=True, E2R.08, thpt_reading_passage
Passage passage-b144c76dc3-04-9f1184f796 (5 questions)
4. At Greenfield High School, students and teachers have joined forces to fight smoking through their "Breathe Free" campaign. The programme started after a school survey showed that many students didn't understand how quickly smoking could damage their health. Now, colourful posters with facts about smoking dangers fill the hallways, and the student council organises monthly "Health Days" with fun activities about clean living. The campaign's most powerful event is the annual "Real Stories" assembly, where former smokers share how cigarettes hurt their lives. Last year, a local basketball player moved students to tears when he described losing his athletic scholarship due to smoking-related health problems. The school nurse also runs a popular "Quit Club" that meets every Thursday, offering support and healthy alternatives like chewing gum or stress balls. Greenfield High School has taken strong steps to enforce no-smoking rules. The school installed new smoke detectors near bathrooms and the back field where students used to sneak cigarettes. Instead of just punishing smokers, the campaign focuses on helping them. Students caught smoking must attend special counselling sessions and participate in creating anti-smoking materials for younger kids. After two years, "Breathe Free" is making a real difference. School health reports show 40% fewer students admitting to smoking, and the student-run "Fresh Air Squad" keeps spreading the message. Even local shops near the school have agreed not to sell cigarettes to Greenfield students. What began as a small health project has grown into a community-wide movement for cleaner lungs and brighter futures.
Q16 Which of the following best summarises paragraph 1?
A. Greenfield High School launched "Breathe Free" to fight smoking with posters and monthly activities | B. Students at Greenfield High School made posters and planned events to prevent smoking among the | C. The “Breathe Free” campaign focused on smoking among students through monthly student-led | D. Students and teachers at Greenfield High School run engaging activities about smoking to raise
Ans: None (missing), ready=True, E2R.08, thpt_reading_passage

Ans: None (missing), ready=True, E2R.08, thpt_reading_passage

In November 2009, they got an apartment together in Palo Alto, California and started work on Pinterest. And it seemed that a lot of people liked their idea. When Pinterest started in March 2010, it grew quickly and had 10 million users after the first year. This was an amazing number compared with Twitter and Facebook: Twitter took two years to reach 10 million users and Facebook took over five years. Just a year after starting, Pinterest was worth $200 million and had over 421 million page views. Silberman's ordinary interest as a boy certainly became an extraordinary business. (Adapted from English Workbook 12)

47MP/CÁC DẠNG CÂU HỎI TRONG ĐỀ THI/ĐỌC HIỂU/Thi Online Cách xử lý câu hỏi tìm đoạn chứa thông tin (Đề số 01).pdfok110 / 0 / 1010 / 10thpt_reading_passage:10missing:10
Passage passage-1696968474-01-1ebf2709d2 (10 questions)
Read the following passages and mark the letter A, B, C, or D on your answer sheet to indicate the best answer to each of the questions. There are many ways to take action to improve your carbon footprint. Choosing to walk, cycle, or use public transport can help reduce pollution and emissions brought on by automobiles. If riding in a car is a must, opt for ride-sharing or carpooling when possible. Air travel is an even worse offender than car travel; if flying is unavoidable, make a commitment to offset your emissions in other ways. You might also accomplish this by modifying your diet. Reducing the consumption of animal products, particularly red meat, will make the biggest impact. But eating locally sourced and seasonal ingredients is also a sustainable practice, as the foodstuffs have less distance to travel. Generally, these foods will also be less processed. Energy is another area where small shifts can make a difference. Opting for fans rather than air conditioning is one example. Keeping your household’s temperature at just 1 degree Celsius less has been shown to reduce both your emissions and your energy bill by 5 to 10 per cent. Turn off lights when they aren’t strictly necessary, and use more sustainable lighting solutions, like LED systems. One larger change you can make is switching to energy providers that rely on renewables like solar or wind power rather than natural gas, coal, and oil. Being a sustainable shopper is also an important part of reducing your carbon footprint. Though many brands take pains to make their production processes more eco-friendly, ultimately, buying less overall and focusing on products that will last is the best remedy. Shopping locally or secondhand rather than at inexpensive retailers producing plastic-heavy garments with unt...

Read the following passages and mark the letter A, B, C, or D on your answer sheet to indicate the best answer to each of the questions. Parent-teenager problems are a common thing in most households. Reasons for problems between teenagers and parents vary greatly, and each situation is different from another, but there are common reasons for parent-teenager problems. First, a teenager is getting bad grades at school. This can cause a problem because the parents of a teen who is not getting the best grades in school will be worried about him graduating from high school and getting into college. Also, if a teen gets bad grades, he probably will not get any school scholarships, which can be a hardship for parents. (Adapted from English 11)

49MP/CÁC DẠNG CÂU HỎI TRONG ĐỀ THI/ĐỌC HIỂU 8 CÂU/Bài 1 Luyện tập bài đọc 8 câu (Buổi 1).pdfok216 / 0 / 1616 / 16thpt_reading_passage:16missing:16
Passage passage-f3af0fad10-01-a0300ecb9a (8 questions)
From a poor British colony to one of Asia's greats, this is Singapore's success story. With not much land or natural resources, Singapore initially had problems with national defence, education, and the medical system. That was until former Prime Minister Lee Kuan Yew began changing Singapore for the better. As a smart man, the former Prime Minister was the key to Singapore's success story. The first thing he did was make Singapore a financial and trading centre. By lowering taxes, and making immigration easier, Singapore attracted a lot of investors. Lee also forced a high transparency policy on taxes and the government. As a result, he created the image of a clean and green Singapore. Lee built the Changi Airport, one of Singapore's most impressive structures, while Singapore was going through the Oil Crisis of 1973. But Lee's risk paid off as Singapore became an economic and travel centre later on. Lee also implemented effective social policies. The Singapore government carried out social policies on housing, education, and medicine, making Singapore one of the top countries on the wellness scale in its first 20 years. What is more, in the early days, Lee made Singapore different from other poor countries with a simple solution: trees. He said not only did trees improve the quality of living but they also prevented the negative effects of urbanisation. (Adapted from THiNK)
Q1 According to paragraph 1, at first, Singapore had to deal with all of the following EXCEPT ________.
A. education | B. natural resources | C. health system | D. national defence
Ans: None (missing), ready=True, E2R.02, thpt_reading_passage

Ans: None (missing), ready=True, E2R.02, thpt_reading_passage

Ans: None (missing), ready=True, E2R.02, thpt_reading_passage

Ans: None (missing), ready=True, E2R.02, thpt_reading_passage

Ans: None (missing), ready=True, E2R.02, thpt_reading_passage

Passage passage-f3af0fad10-02-865b1118e4 (8 questions)
Despite cultural diversity, the primary role of a family in society is to foster an environment for children to learn skills, morals, and values. Families provide initial socialisation for children that shapes their self-worth, attitudes, values and behaviours. In every family, parents and grandparents not only educate children about ethics and family culture but also show them good attitudes, polite gestures and behaviours as well as how to respect the elderly and care for the young. The family factor is primarily responsible for the socialisation of children, which greatly affects children's personalities. Throughout the development stages, children are nurtured and brought up at home. Although at that time, the personality is not clearly shown, children usually imitate what their parents do. Many countries have different special holidays dedicated to mothers, fathers, children and family as a whole. Việt Nam also has such a holiday, Family Day, which is annually celebrated on June 28. Although Vietnamese Family Day is not a public holiday, it is very popular and is celebrated across the whole country. The holiday was first established by The Prime Minister of Việt Nam in 2001. Family Day is a chance for society to join hands together to fight against domestic violence, inequality in marriage, and prevent child abuse. By doing so, it promotes good deeds and beautiful gestures of family members. Nowadays, Vietnamese Family Day is an opportunity for young adults to look back to their roots, relatives, and cultivate the noble feelings and cultural values of the nation. (Adapted from C21 Smart)

50MP/CÁC DẠNG CÂU HỎI TRONG ĐỀ THI/ĐỌC HIỂU 8 CÂU/Bài 2 Luyện tập bài đọc 8 câu (Buổi 2).pdfok216 / 0 / 1616 / 16thpt_reading_passage:16missing:16
Passage passage-340cf9ee82-01-a6d955db0b (8 questions)
Problems are a part of life. Arguments can happen with friends and family over a variety of issues. What is important, though, is how you deal with problems. Arguments with friends can make you feel bad or even angry. It is important to explain your feelings without blaming the other person. Share how you feel by using "I" sentences. For example, don't say "You are always telling me what to do," but "I feel annoyed when you don't listen to me." To solve a problem, you must also listen to how the other person feels and accept that he or she may see a problem differently. Showing your understanding of the other person makes them want to solve the problem. In the end, what is important is to find a solution and become friends again. Families can also have problems. The generation gap can cause parents and children to see things differently, making both feel confused or disappointed. Remember that parents and older family members have years of life experience and want the best for you, so listen to what they say. When you have a problem, try to set a time to sit down and talk quietly. Breathe and count to ten if you start to feel angry or anxious. Be prepared not to get exactly what you want but to find a solution everyone can agree on. We value our families and can solve any problems through caring and communication. (Adapted from Bright)
Q1 The word blaming in paragraph 2 is OPPOSITE in meaning to ______.
A. complimenting | B. helping | C. accusing | D. ignoring
Ans: None (missing), ready=True, E2R.02, thpt_reading_passage

Ans: None (missing), ready=True, E2R.02, thpt_reading_passage

Ans: None (missing), ready=True, E2R.02, thpt_reading_passage

Passage passage-340cf9ee82-02-42d9adb25a (8 questions)
From melting arctic ice caps to forest fires, global warming is heating up the planet and damaging the Earth's ecosystems. Global warming is the rise of the Earth's temperatures caused by an increase of CO2 and other greenhouse gas emissions. Global warming has increased very much in the past 100 years because people are using more and more electricity and driving more cars. We are also cutting down more trees that clean the air. Scientists have been studying the effects of global warming on different ecosystems for a long time and what they have found is quite worrying. The rising temperatures have caused many problems in ecosystems. Arctic ice is melting, causing sea levels to rise and floods in seaside areas. As a result, animals and plants are losing their homes. As oceans and rivers become warmer, the water temperatures keep changing. Some marine animals cannot live in these new conditions. Since the 1970s, populations of fish in lakes and rivers have decreased by 76 percent. Global warming is also causing extreme weather events. Other ecosystems, like deserts and grasslands, are experiencing terrible droughts and certain wildlife and plant species that depend on water are dying. Due to the high temperatures, wildfires are destroying whole ecosystems such as forests. There has been a serious rise in endangered species in recent years that could result in many animals becoming extinct. The loss of plants and animals causes problems for all living things in an ecosystem. We need to take action to stop global warming and save the Earth's ecosystems before it's too late. (Adapted from Bright)
Q9 Which of the following is NOT mentioned as a caused of global warming in paragraph 1?
A. People are cleaning the air. | B. People are consuming more electricity. | C. People are using more cars for travel. | D. People are cutting down more trees.
Ans: None (missing), ready=True, E2R.02, thpt_reading_passage

A. promising | B. confusing | C. typical | D. natural
Ans: None (missing), ready=True, E2R.02, thpt_reading_passage

Ans: None (missing), ready=True, E2R.02, thpt_reading_passage

The main result of high temperatures is the increase in forested areas in ecosystems. | D. Wildfires are causing high temperatures to spread across ecosystems like forests.
Ans: None (missing), ready=True, E2R.02, thpt_reading_passage

Ans: None (missing), ready=True, E2R.02, thpt_reading_passage

Fish populations declined by 76 percent in lakes and rivers during the 1970s. | D. The loss of plants and animals has a negative impact on all living things in an ecosystem.

51MP/CÁC DẠNG CÂU HỎI TRONG ĐỀ THI/ĐỌC HIỂU 8 CÂU/Bài 3 Luyện tập bài đọc 8 câu (Buổi 3).pdfok216 / 0 / 1616 / 16thpt_reading_passage:16missing:16
Passage passage-0b60148d4d-01-90ecc24771 (8 questions)
Many people have learning disorders. They suffer in school: other children bully them or make fun of them and call them 'slow'. However, people can get over their learning disorders and have successful careers with the help and support of family, teachers, and doctors, as these examples prove. At school, Michael Phelps could not sit still and found it difficult to concentrate. But then he found his passion for sports. His reading improved when he read books about sports and when the problems reflected his interests. Through hard work and never missing a day's practice, Michael became an Olympic champion, winning twenty-eight medals, twenty-three of them gold. In the world of autism, Dr Temple Grandin is an important voice. Autistic herself, she is an expert on how to teach people with autism, and she has written six books about the condition. School was difficult for her because she didn't think in the same way as other children. At school, her science teacher became her mentor. With his help and support, Grandin became interested in animal science. She later became a professor of animal science at Colorado State University. Keira Knightley found out she had dyslexia at the age of six. She believes that her problems with reading and writing made her strong. Knightley struggled with reading and writing, but she dreamt of being an actress, so she had to read scripts. She had to work harder than other children to improve her literacy. Later she finished school with top grades. So don't worry if you are different - you never know where your differences could lead you. (Adapted from English Discovery)

A. children | B. careers | C. examples | D. people
Ans: None (missing), ready=True, E2R.02, thpt_reading_passage

Passage passage-0b60148d4d-02-81e818da97 (8 questions)
Sapa has become a favourite destination for eco-tourists. This remote part of Việt Nam is 315 km northwest of Hà Nội, near the Việt Nam-China border. Sapa is famous for the beautiful landscapes of Fansipan Mountain as well as rainforests and rice fields. The Hàm Rồng area of Sapa is 1,800 meters above sea level, so it is cool and full of fresh air. One thing that you should not miss in Sapa is the Hàm Rồng flower garden. It is full of bright-coloured flowers. You can take a 30-minute walk from it to the top of the mountain. Another place to see is Fansipan, known as "The Roof of Indochina". It is the highest mountain in the Hoàng Liên Sơn Range. The height of the mountain is 3,143 meters. Fansipan is only 9 km from Sapa town. You can join a trekking tour. It's not an easy trek, but the view from the top of the mountain is worth trying. Trekkers can see beautiful fields of flowers and rainforests. These wilderness areas have not been deforested, so they remain their natural beauty. Don't forget the locals in Sapa. There are interesting villagers with unique lifestyles. Therefore, spending time in the local villages on your trip is highly recommended. Please remember that Sapa is beautiful, and we want to keep it that way. So please follow the guidelines that your ecotour leader gives you. We want others to enjoy this special place too. (Adapted from English Discovery)

A. It is famous for its beautiful landscapes. | B. It is cool and full of fresh air. | C. It is a tourist attraction. | D. It is an isolated urban area.

52MP/CÁC DẠNG CÂU HỎI TRONG ĐỀ THI/ĐỌC HIỂU 8 CÂU/Bài 4 Luyện tập bài đọc 8 câu (Buổi 4).pdfok216 / 0 / 1616 / 16thpt_reading_passage:16missing:16
Passage passage-013d593d2e-01-7320908cac (8 questions)
Modern supermarkets give us many choices. There are colourful fruits and vegetables, fresh fish and meat, many kinds of bread, eggs, yoghourt, and cheese. But at the same time, people in the modern world are suffering from health problems. Could the food we eat be causing some of these problems? One explanation for modern health problems is that they began when human beings became farmers about 12,000 years ago. Around that time, people started growing and eating crops, such as wheat and rice. Some people believe that returning to an earlier way of eating a "paleo diet" that includes only meat, fish, and fruits and vegetables-might be a solution to our modern health problems. A paleo diet is special because it doesn't include grains, dairy foods, or legumes, such as peanuts or beans. For many of us, it is not possible to prepare our favourite dishes without these ingredients. But people who follow a paleo diet only eat foods people ate before farming began. They believe this is a more natural and healthier way to eat. For Dr. Peter Ungar, the truth is not so simple. In human history, people have eaten a wide variety of foods. Early human beings lived in different places with different climates, so many kinds of food were available to our ancestors even before farming began. Dr. Ungar says our ability to eat in many different ways and to be healthy whether we live in the Arctic or in the tropics is the important thing. One thing is certain, though: If you do decide to try a paleo diet, remember that your meals might actually have less variety than the meals of your early ancestors. (Adapted from Explore New Worlds)

A. abandoning | B. experiencing | C. requiring | D. accessing
Ans: None (missing), ready=True, E2R.02, thpt_reading_passage

Ans: None (missing), ready=True, E2R.02, thpt_reading_passage

Ans: None (missing), ready=True, E2R.02, thpt_reading_passage

Ans: None (missing), ready=True, E2R.02, thpt_reading_passage

Ans: None (missing), ready=True, E2R.02, thpt_reading_passage

Passage passage-013d593d2e-02-1509a8a923 (8 questions)
Walking is a great way to get around. For short trips, for exercise, or just for fun, walking can be better than driving or riding. But in many cities, walking can also be dangerous. Cars, trucks, and motorcycles are a danger to pedestrians, and sometimes there are accidents. David Engwicht, from Brisbane, Australia, wants to do something about this. His book, Reclaiming Our Cities and Towns, has a simple message: We need to take back our streets and make them better places for walking. In the past, Engwicht says, streets belonged to everybody. Children played there, and people walked to work or to stores. Now, however, most city engineers design streets for vehicles. People stay inside buildings to get away from the crowded sidewalks, the noisy streets, and the dangerous traffic. Unfortunately, this gives them less contact with their neighbours. Many cities are working to make their streets safer for pedestrians. There are new crosswalks on the streets and more traffic lights and bicycle lanes. The city of Florence, Italy, only allows cars and buses with special permits to drive on its historic city streets. In Boston, US, the Slow Streets Programme gives some neighbourhoods more stop signs and a 20 mph (32 kph) speed limit. Engwicht travels around the world, helping people think differently about pedestrians, streets, and neighbourhoods. Whether we live in a small town or a city with a population in the millions, Engwicht says we should think of streets as our "outdoor living room." Changing the traffic is just the beginning. In the future, streets may again be safe places for people, and walking will be an even better form of transportation than it is now. (Adapted from Explore New Worlds)

Ans: None (missing), ready=True, E2R.02, thpt_reading_passage

53MP/CÁC DẠNG CÂU HỎI TRONG ĐỀ THI/ĐỌC HIỂU 8 CÂU/Bài 5 Luyện tập bài đọc 8 câu (Buổi 5).pdfok216 / 0 / 1616 / 16thpt_reading_passage:16missing:16
Passage passage-fc399e91bd-01-60472f6619 (8 questions)
When Guy Goma went for a job interview at the BBC, he was very astonished by the interviewer' questions. He'd applied for a job in the computing department. Why did the woman want him to answer questions about a legal case involving a famous music company? He was even more shocked when he found that he was answering these questions live on television. Mr Goma had been waiting in a reception room at Television Centre when an assistant came up and asked him if he was Guy. When he said ‘yes', she told him to follow her to a TV studio. The next thing he knew, he was on national television. At the same time, Guy Kewney, the editor of Newswireless.net, sat in another reception room wondering why he was waiting so long. Mr Goma did his best to answer 'very stressful’ questions from the BBC business journalist Karen Bowerman. When Karen asked him to talk about the result of the court case, he said that it was ‘a big surprise'. He then went on to say that more and more people were downloading music from the Internet because it was very easy. At that point Karen Bowerman appeared to realise something was wrong and very professionally ended the interview after only three questions. The video of Mr Goma was quickly uploaded onto YouTube, where it immediately went viral. Twenty minutes after his TV appearance, Mr Goma finally got to his real interview. Unfortunately, he did not get the job. (Adapted from THiNK)

Passage passage-fc399e91bd-02-02c8bf7a14 (8 questions)
Rachel Carson, an ecologist, a biologist, and a writer, was born in 1907 in Springdale, Pennsylvania. As a small girl, she was an avid reader and soon showed a keen interest in the natural world and writing. She had her first story about animals published in a magazine when she was in the fourth grade. She graduated from Pennsylvania College for Women (now Chatham University) in 1929, studied at the Woods Hole Marine Biological Laboratory, and received her MA in zoology from Johns Hopkins University in 1932. Initially, Rachel Carson worked for the US Bureau of Fisheries as a part-time science writer. Part of her job was to interest the public in marine and freshwater biology via radio programmes. She then spent the next few years serving as a marine scientist and editor for the US Fish and Wildlife Service. There, she became famous for her writings on environmental pollution and the natural history of the sea, in which she always concerned herself about the impact that humans made on the natural world. Climate change, rising sea-levels, melting Arctic glaciers, decreasing animal populations are part of her work. In her most influential book, Silent Spring (1962), Rachel Carson strongly disapproved of the widespread use of pesticides such as DDT. She warned the public about its negative long-term effects and called for new policies to protect humans and the environment. She then was criticised by the chemical industry, but never gave up. The book eventually prompted a change in national policy, leading to the enactment of a national ban of pesticides on agricultural farming. Additionally, it helped spark the environmental movement, resulting in the establishment of the United States Environmental Protection Agency. That's why she was called the mother of modern ecology. ...

Ans: None (missing), ready=True, E2R.02, thpt_reading_passage

54MP/CÁC DẠNG CÂU HỎI TRONG ĐỀ THI/ĐỌC HIỂU 10 CÂU/Luyện tập Đọc hiểu 10 câu hỏi (Buổi 1).pdfok220 / 0 / 2020 / 20thpt_reading_passage:20missing:20
Passage passage-ac6ddaa10c-01-97b620b06a (10 questions)
Ben Silbermann was an ordinary boy. He was born in 1982 in Iowa in the USA and he loved collecting things; stamps, leaves, and especially insects. As a young man in his 20s, his love of collecting turned into big business. [I] He went to Yale University and studied chemistry and political science. [II] In 2006, when he graduated from university, he moved to the state of California and got a job at Google. [III] Silbermann met a lot of people there who were very interested in technology, and he decided on two things. [IV] He worked at Google for two years. Then, in 2008, Silbermann left his job and he started his own business with two friends: Paul Sciarra and Evan Sharp. In November 2009, they got an apartment together in Palo Alto, California and started work on Pinterest. Silbermann and Sharp realised they both loved collecting, and they made a "virtual pinboard" - pages online where you "pin" pictures of things you like and things you "collect". Their idea was to connect everyone in the world through the things they find interesting. And it seemed that a lot of people liked their idea. When Pinterest started in March 2010, it grew quickly and had 10 million users after the first year. This was an amazing number compared with Twitter and Facebook: Twitter took two years to reach 10 million users and Facebook took over five years. Just a year after starting, Pinterest was worth $200 million and had over 421 million page views. Silbermann's ordinary interest as a boy certainly became an extraordinary business. (Adapted from English 12)

Passage passage-ac6ddaa10c-02-e13ad8a519 (10 questions)
Modern electronic devices might look clean on the outside, but inside they contain a lot of materials used in manufacture which may be dangerous to human health. Many countries have refused to make the investment in waste-handling equipment and simply shipped their e-waste abroad, usually to developing nations. The latest United Nations Environment Programme (UNEP) report estimates that, worldwide, electronic waste is increasing by about 40 million tons a year. So what can we do about it? The first thing to do is recognise the problem. [I] We have gradually come to realise that in two ways in particular, modern hi-tech can be bad for the planet. [II] The first is its energy use; the worldwide scale of information technology is so enormous that electronics now produce fully two per cent of global carbon emissions. [III] The other is the hardware, when it comes to the end of its natural life. [IV] The European Union has recognised the problem by adopting a key principle: producer responsibility. In other words, making it the duty of manufacturers of electronic goods to ensure their safe disposal at the end of their lives. It also suggests that all countries should start to establish proper e-waste management networks, which could both cut down on health problems, generate employment and reduce greenhouse gas emissions. Groups such as Greenpeace have led the way in putting pressure on major manufacturing companies to find substitutes for the toxic chemicals inside their products. Encouragingly, they have had some success in forcing them to develop non-poisonous alternatives to these. This may be the real way forward. (Adapted from Compact First)

Ans: None (missing), ready=True, E2R.02, thpt_reading_passage

55MP/CÁC DẠNG CÂU HỎI TRONG ĐỀ THI/ĐỌC HIỂU 10 CÂU/Luyện tập Đọc hiểu 10 câu hỏi (Buổi 2.pdfok220 / 0 / 2020 / 20thpt_reading_passage:20missing:20
Passage passage-665b239786-01-3da0f89297 (10 questions)
In China, it is believed that tai chi can delay ageing and prolong life, increase flexibility, strengthen muscles and tendons, and aid in the treatment of heart disease, high blood pressure, arthritis, skin diseases, depression, cancer, and many other diseases. Most of the research on tai chi has been done in older individuals in the area of balance and fall prevention. This area of research is important because fall-related injuries are the leading cause of death from injury and disability among older adults. Because tai chi movements are slow and deliberate with shifts of body weight from one leg to the other in coordination with upper body movements, it challenges balance and many have long assumed it helps improve balance and reduce fall frequency. [I] In a similar tai chi study of older adults, 54% of the subjects who practised tai chi attributed their improved sense of confidence to improved balance. [II] One study looked at adults in their 60s and 70s who practised tai chi three times a week for 12 weeks (60-minute classes). [III] After just six weeks, statistically significant improvements were observed in balance, muscular strength, endurance, and flexibility measures. [IV] The demands of living are stressful for people of all ages. Although one cannot directly point to studies showing a reduction in stress from practising tai chi, the breathing, movement, and mental concentration required of individuals who practise tai chi may be just the distraction you need from your hectic lifestyle. (Adapted from English 11)
Q1 All of the following are mentioned in paragraph 1 as the benefít of practising tai chi EXCEPT ________.
A. strengthen muscles | B. increase life expectancy | C. improve flexibility | D. prevent the process of ageing
Ans: None (missing), ready=True, E2R.02, thpt_reading_passage

Passage passage-665b239786-02-91b91f40bf (10 questions)
The generation gap refers to the difference in attitudes or behaviour between a younger generation and the older one. Different attitudes towards life, different views about certain problems, and a lack of communication can cause a generation gap in most societies. The first factor is that parents and children generally see things from different perspectives. [I] Choosing a career is a good example for this. [II] Many parents try to impose certain careers they favour on their children, based on their own perceptions. [III] However, young people don't always understand their parents' points of views. [IV] Another factor lies in differences in musical tastes, fashion, and political views between young people and their parents. For example, having a pierced nose might be viewed as fashionable by some teens, but for many parents, it is considered to be an act of rebellion against social norms. Some parents may also find their children's behaviour unacceptable and disrespectful to traditional values. The major factor causing the generation gap is the lack of communication between parents and their children. Parents tend to be dominant and not to trust their children to deal with crises. Also, they keep talking too much about certain problems. That is the reason why young people seldom reveal their feelings to their parents. When facing problems, young people prefer to seek help from their classmates or friends. The above mentioned factors could lead to a generation gap. In order to bridge the gap between the old generation and the younger one, mutual understanding is the vital key. The wisest solution would be for parents and their children to treat each other as friends. (Adapted from English 11)
Q11 As stated in paragraph 1, all of the following can lead to the generation gap EXCEPT __________.
A. different views on specific issues | B. a lack of communication | C. technological advances | D. different attitudes towards life
Ans: None (missing), ready=True, E2R.02, thpt_reading_passage

56MP/CÁC DẠNG CÂU HỎI TRONG ĐỀ THI/ĐỌC HIỂU 10 CÂU/Luyện tập Đọc hiểu 10 câu hỏi (Buổi 3).pdfok220 / 0 / 2020 / 20thpt_reading_passage:20missing:20
Passage passage-9a5922cb4d-01-abf3a487fa (10 questions)
Being independent is being able to take care of yourself, and not having to rely on anyone else. That is what many young people strive for. However, the ability to live independently does not develop naturally: you need a number of life skills to stop relying on your parents and older siblings. Among those skills, time management is probably the most important one. With good time- management skills, you can build your confidence and self-esteem. These skills will also help you to perform your daily tasks, including your responsibilities at school and at home. If you can use your time wisely, you will not feel very stressed when exam dates are approaching. You can act more independently and responsibly, get better grades at school and have more time for your family and friends. Time-management skills are not difficult to develop. [I] First, make plans for the things you need to do on a planner or an app on your mobile device. [II] Organise them in a schedule so that they can be checked later. Second, prioritise your activities. [III] If you have so much to do in a day or a week, you may be at a loss as to how to fit everything in. [IV] So decide what is important to you and give it the most of your time or add it to the top of your list. Third, develop routines, because once routines are developed, they take less time to do. Time-management skills cannot be developed in one day. However, when you have them, they can help you to become independent. (Adapted from English 11)

Ans: None (missing), ready=True, E2R.02, thpt_reading_passage

Passage passage-9a5922cb4d-02-513843a1db (10 questions)
Rapid advances in robotic applications for domestic and personal use have made intelligent robots become part of our everyday lives. In order to help robot designers and developers to imagine a road map for their development in the future, there have been surveys conducted in many countries to study people's attitudes towards robots, with a particular focus on domestic use. Globally, the results show that a large proportion of people in the surveys have a positive attitude towards the idea of having intelligent service robots as small 'domestic machines'. These machines can be 'controlled' and do mainly difficult and repetitive household tasks such as cleaning, ironing, and cooking. A few want robots to be more like friends and have human-like abilities such as the ability to speak and communicate with them. However, these people do not want robots to have human appearance or to substitute humans in child or animal care because they regard these tasks as unsuitable for machines. [I] On the other hand, many people have a negative attitude towards robots. [II] The biggest fear is that humans might lose control over robots. [III] All these ideas seem to come from a typical science- fiction scenario where robots get out of control by gaining too much autonomy. [IV] Having positive or negative attitudes towards intelligent robots is not related to where the interviewees live. A majority of them are in countries where they have daily contact with robots in various industries or businesses, and see them on TV, in films, or in their immediate environment. Most of them believe that the day robots will become part of our lives will come sooner or later. (Adapted from English 12)

A. proposal | B. attempt | C. preparation | D. plan
Ans: None (missing), ready=True, E2R.02, thpt_reading_passage

Ans: None (missing), ready=True, E2R.02, thpt_reading_passage

57MP/CÁC DẠNG CÂU HỎI TRONG ĐỀ THI/ĐỌC HIỂU 10 CÂU/Luyện tập Đọc hiểu 10 câu hỏi (Buổi 4).pdfok220 / 0 / 2020 / 20thpt_reading_passage:20missing:20
Passage passage-48d778f6b9-01-fd6fafcc82 (10 questions)
My name is Harry and I live in Bath, a small beautiful city in the south-west of England. I left school when I was 18, after taking my A levels. My results were not great, but probably high enough for university. However, I am not very academic so I decided not to study for a degree. I thought there would be lots of companies looking for bright school leavers who want to head straight into the world of work through an apprenticeship. Money was also a problem. Although there were tuition loans available and my parents were willing to help me financially, I didn't want to borrow money and then graduate from university with a huge debt. I started looking for an apprenticeship and it took me several months to get my first interview. [I] My application was not successful; there were more than 4,000 applicants and only 20 were selected. [II] I realised that apprenticeships were very competitive so I started to look for other jobs. [III] I worked as a barista at coffee shops, interviewed people on the street, sold tickets for events, and was even a human statue at different theme parks and festivals. [IV] Then while I was working at one of the music festivals, I had an idea. Why not start up a mobile catering business? Buying a small trailer didn't require a large investment and finding someone to cook was easy. Now I have a team of 15 people, travel to fantastic events around the country, sell delicious food, and then watch people enjoy it. Although I didn't get my ideal apprenticeship in software development or accountancy, I am a successful young entrepreneur. (Adapted from English 12)
Q1 What is NOT indicated about Harry in paragraph 1?
A. He lives in a small city in England. | B. He left school after completing his A levels. | C. His A-level results were likely enough for university. | D. He couldn’t afford to study for a degree.
Ans: None (missing), ready=True, E2R.02, thpt_reading_passage

Passage passage-48d778f6b9-02-4704102c11 (10 questions)
'Lifelong learning' is the pursuit of knowledge throughout life. [I] In other words, learning is not confined to the classroom environment and school subjects. [II] Instead, people can learn throughout their adulthood, even in old age and in a variety of situations, particularly in their daily interactions with others and with the world around them. [III] Therefore, lifelong learners should have a strong desire to learn and explore the world. [IV] Lifelong learning is facilitated by e-learning platforms. Education is now no longer offered only by 'bricks and mortar' institutions. Online courses have instead enabled lifelong learning by providing learners, teachers, and course providers with much more flexibility in terms of learning time, place, pace, and style. Learners, for instance, can now have the course content taught to them by a school located miles away from where they live or work. In addition, young parents can select their group meeting time late in the evening when their children have gone to bed. Most importantly, lifelong learning should be regarded as an attitude to learning for self- improvement rather than a pathway to qualifications. This means the ultimate aim is to better yourself for personal and/or professional development rather than for some certificate to decorate your CV. This type of lifelong learning may not necessarily take place as part of a course. It may be done very informally in any area of interest and at your convenience, as long as it is well within your capabilities and/or improves your skills. For example, researching the tidal cycles of the sea where you often go fishing and the types of fish available there is a form of lifelong learning. Lifelong learning also fosters adaptability in a rapidly changing world. In today’s fast-pa...

Ans: None (missing), ready=True, E2R.02, thpt_reading_passage

58MP/CÁC DẠNG CÂU HỎI TRONG ĐỀ THI/ĐỌC HIỂU 10 CÂU/Luyện tập Đọc hiểu 10 câu hỏi (Buổi 5).pdfok220 / 0 / 2020 / 20thpt_reading_passage:20missing:20
Passage passage-a776f35fc8-01-c9e4dbae2b (10 questions)
Every day, there are news reports about natural disasters, like floods, earthquakes, and hurricanes in the media. With extreme weather conditions occurring more and more often, you may want to be ready for them. There are three important things you should do at home to prepare. In general, the first thing is to stock up on emergency supplies. [I] You should keep enough food and water for at least three days in your house. [II] Choose food that can be stored for a long time and food that can be eaten without cooking. [III] You can also store dry foods like crackers and nuts. [IV] On top of that, be sure to store food that you like, and include a few special treats like candy or cookies. In an emergency situation, it's nice to have something to cheer you up. As well as that, it is important to include any special foods that babies, small children, or elderly people in your family may need. Another thing is water: be sure to keep plenty of it. Each person needs one gallon of water per day for drinking and basic washing. Generally speaking, emergencies can occur in every country in the world, but it's important to think about the equipment you might need for the kinds of disasters that are more likely in your country. You might need blankets, very warm clothes, flashlights, or plastic bags. In other words, think about possible situations, and buy the things that would be needed to cope with them. In addition, plan what you will do in case you need to evacuate your home. In particular, decide where you will go and actually arrange a meeting place in advance, such as a relative's home or a big public building. Also, be sure that all family members have the phone number of a contact person in another city. In short, have a plan to find your family members if you become separat...

Passage passage-a776f35fc8-02-109020182f (10 questions)
The astonishing speed of technological development makes it necessary for individuals to learn new skills and keep improving them throughout their lives. The term lifelong learning (LLL) refers to the continuous accumulation of an individual's knowledge and skills. Apart from the knowledge, skills, attitudes, and behaviours people acquire in their everyday lives, LLL consists of three forms: formal, non-formal, and informal learning. Formal learning is learning that happens in formal and organised settings like schools and universities, and very often leads to certificates or degrees. While non-formal learning is considered learning gained in planned activities like vocational skills training, informal learning is learning acquired from daily life activities. LLL offers a great number of benefits to individuals, communities, and the country. First of all, it brings individuals knowledge, enhances their existing skills, stimulates learning experiences, and builds up systems of values, necessary for them as social human beings. [I] In addition, knowledgeable individuals make communities more productive and creative with their up-to-date skills, abilities, and ideas. [II] Today's fast-changing knowledge economy requires workers who are well-prepared for new challenges by constant learning. [III] An able, skilful, and knowledgeable person can enormously contribute to their country's development and prosperity, which in turn contributes to the happiness and well-being of its citizens. [IV] To truly embrace LLL, individuals should cultivate curiosity and openness to new experiences. Whether through exploring hobbies, pursuing online courses, or engaging in community projects, maintaining a mindset of continuous growth fosters adaptability and resilience in a rapidly changing ...

151MP/LUYỆN GIẢI ĐỀ THI TỐT NGHIỆP/CHINH PHỤC CÂU HỎI 10 ĐIỂM/SUMMARY/Luyện tập chuyên sâu tư duy xử lý câu hỏi summary (Buổi 1).pdfok115 / 0 / 1515 / 15thpt_reading_passage:15missing:15
Passage passage-f73987984f-01-0d702b20ae (15 questions)
KỸ NĂNG & CHIẾN THUẬT XỬ LÝ CÂU HỎI SUMMARY LUYỆN TẬP CHUYÊN SÂU TƯ DUY XỬ LÝ CÂU HỎI SUMMARY (BUỔI 1) Plus 2: Chinh phục câu hỏi 10 điểm Reading Passage 1 Urban sprawl has become a defining issue for many fast-growing cities around the world. As populations rise and housing demand increases, urban areas often expand outward rather than upward. This outward growth may seem like a natural response to overcrowded centres, but it raises important questions about sustainability, public planning, and the long-term well-being of communities. At first glance, suburban expansion appears attractive: land on the outskirts is cheaper, families can find larger homes, and residents enjoy a quieter environment away from congestion. However, these benefits come with hidden costs. When development spreads over a wide area, cities must extend roads, power lines, and public services across long distances. This dispersed pattern makes daily life more dependent on private vehicles, contributing to heavier traffic, greater fuel consumption, and higher carbon emissions. As a result, what seems like a convenient lifestyle can gradually create environmental pressure. Urban sprawl also reshapes social dynamics. Low-density neighbourhoods often reduce opportunities for public interaction because shops, schools, and workplaces are located far apart. Many residents feel disconnected, spending hours commuting rather than engaging with their local community. In some regions, sprawl accelerates the loss of farmland and natural habitats, pushing wildlife to the margins and weakening the ecological balance around metropolitan zones. Critics argue that once land is converted into endless subdivisions, it becomes extremely difficult to restore. To address these challenges, many cities are adopting more c...

152MP/LUYỆN GIẢI ĐỀ THI TỐT NGHIỆP/CHINH PHỤC CÂU HỎI 10 ĐIỂM/SUMMARY/Luyện tập chuyên sâu tư duy xử lý câu hỏi summary (Buổi 2).pdfok115 / 0 / 1515 / 15thpt_reading_passage:15missing:15
Passage passage-ba46170708-01-93492967f3 (15 questions)
KỸ NĂNG & CHIẾN THUẬT XỬ LÝ CÂU HỎI SUMMARY LUYỆN TẬP CHUYÊN SÂU TƯ DUY XỬ LÝ CÂU HỎI SUMMARY (BUỔI 2) Plus 2: Chinh phục câu hỏi 10 điểm Reading Passage 1 Festivals, universally recognised as periods of communal joy and cultural expression, are deeply embedded in societies worldwide. These events are intended to strengthen social bonds and honour traditions, providing a vital sense of identity and continuity. However, the scale and methods of contemporary celebrations are increasingly generating severe ecological consequences. The very practices designed to foster life and community are, paradoxically, contributing to environmental degradation on an alarming scale, transforming occasions of reverence into sources of profound pollution. A prime illustration of this disturbing trend can be observed in the Indian capital, New Delhi, following major festivals like Diwali. The city, already notorious for its poor air quality, experiences a catastrophic deterioration in its atmosphere. The combination of fireworks during Diwali and the subsequent burning of agricultural waste in neighbouring states creates a toxic cocktail of pollutants. The concentration of particulate matter, specifically PM2.5, frequently skyrockets to levels multiple times beyond what global health authorities deem safe, plunging the metropolis into a hazardous smog. The immediate repercussions are a public health emergency. The dense, poisonous smog triggers respiratory ailments, eye irritations, and exacerbates cardiovascular conditions, leading to a surge in hospital admissions. The visual and ecological impact is equally devastating. Water bodies, such as the Yamuna River, become choked with non-biodegradable waste, religious offerings, and chemical residues from idols and paints. This pollution deva...

THI ONLINE – LUYỆN TẬP CHUYÊN SÂU TƯ DUY XỬ LÝ CÂU HỎI SUMMARY (BUỔI 1) Plus 2: Chinh phục câu hỏi 10 điểm Reading Passage 1 We inhabit an era defined by a pervasive throwaway lifestyle, a socio-economic paradigm that prioritises transient convenience over enduring quality. This mentality, deeply ingrained in consumerist societies, champions the single-use, the rapidly obsolete, and the easily replaceable. From ephemeral fast- fashion garments to technologically obsolescent smartphones, products are designed with planned obsolescence, ensuring a deliberately limited lifespan to guarantee repurchase. This cycle is fueled by a powerful, globally orchestrated marketing engine that conflates novelty with progress and consumption with fulfilment, fostering a perpetual state of desire and discard. The detrimental ramifications of this linear "take-make-dispose" model are profound and multifaceted. Environmentally, it generates a staggering deluge of waste, overwhelming landfills and leaching toxic chemicals into ecosystems. The extraction of raw materials and the energy-intensive production processes exacerbate resource depletion and carbon emissions. Socially, this system often perpetuates inequitable labour practices in manufacturing hubs, where low-cost production is paramount. Moreover, it cultivates a superficial relationship with our possessions, eroding the values of maintenance, repair, and the appreciation of craftsmanship, thereby impoverishing our material culture. Counteracting this entrenched model requires a fundamental reconceptualisation of value and ownership. The burgeoning principles of the circular economy present a cogent alternative, emphasising durability, repairability, and resource recuperation. This entails supporting ethically-minded brands that cha...

Passage passage-9cff1b9cc0-01-edcce01ca8 (15 questions)
THI ONLINE – LUYỆN TẬP CHUYÊN SÂU TƯ DUY XỬ LÝ CÂU HỎI SUMMARY (BUỔI 2) Plus 2: Chinh phục câu hỏi 10 điểm Reading Passage 1 The universal childhood experience of losing one's first baby tooth is far from a merely biological event; it is a profound rite of passage that cultures around the world have enshrouded in unique and meaningful tradition. These customs, passed down through generations, transmute a moment of minor physical detachment into a rich narrative, connecting children to ancestral beliefs, supernatural entities, and communal values. The handling of the deciduous tooth thus becomes a microcosm of a culture’s worldview, reflecting themes of reward, protection, and symbolic transition. In many Western nations, a predominantly secular yet whimsical figure—the Tooth Fairy— pervades the custom. This ethereal being is said to visit sleeping children, surreptitiously exchanging the placed tooth for a coin or small gift. This tradition, while commercialised, inculcates early lessons on patience and the rewards of relinquishing a part of oneself. It sanitises the potentially frightening loss with a benevolent fantasy, transforming anxiety into anticipatory excitement. Conversely, numerous cultures ascribe the ritual to anthropomorphic creatures with distinct characteristics. In Spain and many Hispanic countries, a small mouse named Ratoncito Pérez or El Ratón de los Dientes performs the exchange. Across parts of Europe and Russia, a sprite-like Tooth Mouse is the quintessential agent. This rodent symbolism is arguably rooted in pragmatism, as mice, with their ever-growing incisors, symbolise strong, regenerative growth—a poignant wish for the child’s incoming permanent teeth. Perhaps the most elaborate and symbolically potent customs originate from Asia. In Korea an...

Passage passage-5d468fc2e8-01-88f090012f (20 questions)
Read the following passage and do the task below. In the dynamic landscape of social media, a curious trend is quietly gaining traction among young people: the practice of posting zero content. Known as “digital lurking” or “zero-posting,” this involves maintaining an active account to consume content, follow trends, and stay connected, while deliberately never sharing personal updates, photos, or opinions. This stands in stark contrast to the previous generation’s impulse to curate and broadcast their lives online meticulously. Several compelling motivations underpin this shift towards online invisibility. A primary driver is a growing consciousness of privacy and data security. Many youths, wary of permanent digital footprints and sophisticated algorithms, choose to minimise their online exposure. Furthermore, the pressure to perform a perfect, aesthetically pleasing life can be exhausting. Abstaining from posting becomes a form of resistance against the anxiety of constant comparison and the performative nature of modern platforms. It is a deliberate rejection of the validation-seeking cycle of likes and comments. The implications of this silent engagement are multifaceted. For individuals, it can foster a healthier relationship with social media, reducing stress and freeing up mental space. However, it also creates a passive audience that consumes without contributing, which can paradoxically reinforce the very influence of the prolific content creators and commercial entities they observe. From a societal perspective, if large segments of a generation disengage from public discourse online, it could subtly reshape the nature of digital communities, making them less about peer interaction and more about one-way broadcasting. Ultimately, the zero-poster trend signifi...

Passage passage-1480ea8aa5-01-292a32ee72 (20 questions)
Read the following passage and do the task below. The modern smart city is characterised by a vast, often invisible network: the ubiquitous presence of Closed-Circuit Television (CCTV) cameras. Mounted on streetlights, building façades, and transport hubs, these unblinking eyes form a pervasive surveillance grid. This infrastructure is promoted as the cornerstone of urban safety and efficiency, utilising sophisticated analytics to monitor traffic flow, deter crime, and manage crowds. However, this transformation of public space into a zone of constant observation raises profound questions about the balance between collective security and individual liberty in the digital age. Proponents argue that such surveillance is a necessary and pragmatic response to contemporary urban challenges. The deployment of high-definition cameras with facial recognition capabilities can, in theory, act as a powerful deterrent to criminal activity and significantly aid law enforcement investigations. Beyond security, the data harvested is instrumental in optimising city functions—easing traffic congestion, coordinating public services, and streamlining emergency responses. This data-driven governance is framed not as oversight, but as a service, creating a more responsive and orderly urban environment for all citizens. Conversely, critics warn of creeping towards a surveillance state, where the presumption of privacy in public space is irrevocably eroded. The aggregation of countless data points—our movements, associations, and routines—creates detailed digital profiles without explicit consent. This risks enabling social control, chilling free assembly and expression, and disproportionately targeting marginalised communities. The very technology hailed for its objectivity can perpetuate an...

Q6 Following are statements about a scientific phenomenon. Which statement can be a fact?
A. This phenomenon is the most fascinating thing in science. | B. This phenomenon occurs when water freezes at 0 degrees Celsius. | C. This phenomenon makes scientists think about the mysteries of nature. | D. Everyone agrees this phenomenon is one of nature’s greatest wonders.

Q7 Your manager notices an error in your report and informs you about it. What is the best way to respond? Manager: There seems to be a mistake in this section. Can you fix it? You: _________

Ans: None (missing), ready=True, E2M.01, hsa_sentence_completion

Q9 Following are statements about a book. Which statement can be a fact?

Q10 You accidentally spill water on a classmate's book during a group study session. What would be the best way to handle the situation?

Ans: None (missing), ready=True, E2M.01, hsa_sentence_completion

Q19 Which of the following best paraphrases the underlined sentence?

Ans: None (missing), ready=True, E2S.03, thpt_arrangement_text

Ans: None (missing), ready=True, E2S.03, thpt_arrangement_text

Ans: None (missing), ready=True, E2S.03, thpt_arrangement_text

Ans: None (missing), ready=True, E2S.03, thpt_arrangement_text

If people had avoided releasing gases for years, ultraviolet radiation would not have existed at all.

538MP/NỀN TẢNG TỪ VỰNG - ĐỌC HIỂU/20 CHỦ ĐỀ TỪ VỰNG VÀ ĐỌC ĐIỀN - ĐỌC HIỂU CƠ BẢN/03. Chủ đề 1 _ Family and Relationships (2).pdfok315 / 0 / 1515 / 15hsa_cloze_text:15missing:15
Passage passage-56c9d1db0c-01-c7955af72c (3 questions)
There are two common types of families: nuclear families and extended families. A nuclear family has parents and their children living in the same house. This type of family is smaller, so life is often quieter and more private. Parents can focus more on their children, and daily routines are easier to manage. Children in nuclear families often have more freedom and space. An extended family includes other relatives such as grandparents, uncles, aunts, or cousins living together. In this type of family, there are more people to share household chores and help each other. Grandparents can take care of the children when parents are busy. However, with many people living in one house, there can be less privacy. Also, different ages and opinions may lead to small arguments. Still, living in an extended family helps build strong family bonds and teaches children to respect older people.
Q1 Which of the following is NOT true about a nuclear family?
A. It usually has fewer people. | B. Children enjoy more personal space. | C. Daily routines are difficult to manage. | D. Parents can give more attention to their kids.
Ans: None (missing), ready=True, E2C.05, hsa_cloze_text
Q2 The word “arguments” in paragraph 2 is CLOSEST in meaning to _______.
A. routines | B. manners | C. bonds | D. conflicts
Ans: None (missing), ready=True, E2C.05, hsa_cloze_text
Q3 Which of the following is TRUE about an extended family?
A. Grandparents teach kids to respect their parents. | B. Children mainly live with their grandparents. | C. There is more space and privacy for each person. | D. Family members can share household chores.
Ans: None (missing), ready=True, E2C.05, hsa_cloze_text
Passage passage-56c9d1db0c-02-55a28dda28 (4 questions)
The generation gap is the difference in opinions, values, and ways of thinking between older and younger people. This gap often happens between parents and their children. It is normal because each generation grows up in a different time, with different experiences, technology, and lifestyles. As a result, they may not always understand each other. Young people today grow up with the internet, smartphones, and social media, while their parents or grandparents did not have these things. This can lead to different views on what is important in life. For example, older people may value hard work and saving money, while younger people may focus more on enjoying life or following their passions. These differences can sometimes cause arguments in families. However, the generation gap can be bridged if both sides try to understand each other. Young people should listen to the advice of older family members, and older people should try to accept new ideas and changes in the world. Talking and sharing more can help build stronger relationships. In the end, both generations can learn from one another and live more happily together.
Q4 According to paragraph 1, the generation gap refers to the difference in all of the following EXCEPT _______.
A. values | B. technology | C. ways of thinking | D. opinions
Ans: None (missing), ready=True, E2C.05, hsa_cloze_text
Q5 The word “their” in paragraph 2 refers to _______.
A. families | B. older people | C. differences | D. younger people
Ans: None (missing), ready=True, E2C.05, hsa_cloze_text
Q6 The word “bridged” in paragraph 2 is CLOSEST in meaning to _______.
A. reduced | B. strengthened | C. respected | D. Experimented
Ans: None (missing), ready=True, E2C.05, hsa_cloze_text
Q7 Which of the following is TRUE according to the passage?
A. Parents and children always have the same views on every aspect of life. | B. The generation gap exists because young people do not respect their grandparents. | C. Talking and sharing more can help narrow the generation gap in a family. | D. Technology is the main reason why the generation gap exists in a family.
Ans: None (missing), ready=True, E2C.05, hsa_cloze_text
Passage passage-56c9d1db0c-03-455237ca30 (8 questions)
Doing household chores is a great way for children to learn important life skills. Tasks like washing clothes, cleaning the house, or helping others teach children how to take care of their future homes. These are skills that schools cannot always teach, so it is important for children to learn them at home. When children share housework, they also learn about responsibility. Even if they don’t enjoy the tasks, they know they must try to finish them. This helps them understand that every person in the family has a job to do, and that working hard is a good habit. Doing chores also helps children feel gratitude toward their parents. They see how much work their parents do each day and begin to respect and thank them more. This makes them more caring and thoughtful at home. Housework can also strengthen family bonds. When parents and children do chores together, they create special moments. Children feel like they are part of a team. In the end, doing housework brings many good things. It builds essential life skills, helps children grow strong, and brings the family closer. Parents should teach and encourage their children to help, for their own good and the good of the whole family.
Q8 Which of the following is NOT mentioned as a type of household chore?
A. helping others | B. washing clothes | C. taking care of future homes | D. cleaning the house
Ans: None (missing), ready=True, E2C.05, hsa_cloze_text
Q9 The word “them” in paragraph 1 refers to ______.
A. skills | B. schools | C. children | D. home
Ans: None (missing), ready=True, E2C.05, hsa_cloze_text

A. interest | B. responsibility | C. disrespect | D. character
Ans: None (missing), ready=True, E2C.05, hsa_cloze_text

A. Doing chores together is the only way for parents and children to have special moments. | B. The best moments between parents and children happen only when they finish chores. | C. Parents and children can enjoy special moments by sharing housework. | D. Special moments are possible only when parents and children do chores together.
Ans: None (missing), ready=True, E2C.05, hsa_cloze_text

Ans: None (missing), ready=True, E2C.05, hsa_cloze_text

A. Schools play the main role in teaching children how to do household chores. | B. If children aren’t asked to do housework, they won’t be thankful for their parents. | C. Doing household chores helps children learn necessary values and life skills. | D. Only by doing household chores together can family bonds be strengthened.
Ans: None (missing), ready=True, E2C.05, hsa_cloze_text
